answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe what caused your elbow pain or any injury to it?
answers.intake_a2: I don't know. It just started when I was golfing.
answers.intake_q3: Can you describe the type of pain you are feeling in your elbow and if it worsens with any specific movements?
answers.intake_a3: sharp pain with moving my forearm backwards
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your hand or fingers?
answers.intake_q6: Any fever, chills, or other signs of infection?
answers.intake_q7: Any prior history of elbow problems or other joint issues?
answers.intake_q8: Can you point to the exact spot on your elbow where the pain is the worst?
answers.intake_q9: Any difficulty gripping or lifting objects with your hand?
answers.intake_q10: Any recent changes in your activity level or new repetitive motions besides golfing?

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 54-year-old female presenting with a one-week history of sharp pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while golfing and worsens with moving the forearm backward. She reports mild swelling and slight tingling but denies numbness, weakness, fever, chills, or prior elbow problems. There is no difficulty gripping or lifting objects, and no recent changes in activity other than golfing.

O: Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: The working diagnosis is lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), given the location of pain on the lateral elbow, onset related to repetitive activity (golfing), and pain exacerbated by forearm movement. 
Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Elbow bursitis 
4. Early osteoarthritis of the elbow. Patient is not pregnant.

P: 
 - First-line medication: Ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6–8 hours as needed for pain, for 7–10 days. Avoid if history of gastrointestinal bleeding or allergy to NSAIDs. 
 - Alternatives: 
 - Naproxen 250–500 mg orally twice daily for 7–10 days; avoid if NSAID allergy or gastrointestinal risk. 
 - Acetaminophen 500–1000 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for pain, max 3 g/day; avoid in severe liver disease. 
 - Medication safety considerations: No known medication allergies, no pregnancy, no renal or liver disease. NSAIDs should be used cautiously if gastrointestinal risk factors develop. 
 - Return precautions: Return for in-person evaluation if pain worsens, swelling increases, new numbness or weakness develops, or if symptoms do not improve within 1–2 weeks. 
 - Testing: If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).

Your elbow joint is made up of bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet:
m ultrasound therapy – where high-frequency sound waves are used to increase blood flow, which can reduce pain and speed up healing Surgery may be an option if you still have tennis elbow after 6 to 12 months.
Causes of tennis elbow 
Tennis elbow is where the tendons in your elbow become inflamed. Tendons are strong cords that connect muscle to bone.
You can get tennis elbow if you do activities that involve gripping something and repeatedly twisting your wrist and forearm.
Activities that can cause tennis elbow include:
computer work, such as typing or using a mouse manual tasks, such as sewing or using a screwdriver leisure activities, such as tennis or playing a musical instrument Anyone can get tennis elbow but it's more common in people between 35 and 54 years of age.
Page last reviewed: 31 May 2024
Next review due: 31 May 2027

evidence.title: Arthritis - NHS
evidence.snippet:
wards polymyalgia rheumatica  – a condition that almost always affects people over 50 years of age, where the immune system causes muscle pain and stiffness, usually across the shoulders and tops of the legs. It can also cause joint inflammation 
Symptoms of arthritis 
There are lots of different types of arthritis.
The symptoms you experience will vary depending on the type you have.
This is why it's important to have an accurate diagnosis if you have:
joint pain, tenderness and stiffness inflammation in and around the joints restricted movement of the joints warm red skin over the affected joint weakness and muscle wasting 
Arthritis and children Arthritis is often associated with older people, but it can also affect children.
Most types of childhood arthritis are known as juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA).
JIA causes pain and inflammation in 1 or more joints for at least 6 weeks.
